mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a deductive system and let \(A\) be an object of \(\mathcal{D}\). A downshift structure for \(A\) is defined to be a positive object \(\mathord{\Downarrow} A\) and a thunkable map \(\mathsf{wrap} _ {A} : A \vdash \mathord{\Downarrow} A\) for which there exists a necessarily unique and linear inverse \(\mathsf{unwrap} _ {A} : \mathord{\Downarrow} A \vdash A\).

Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a deductive system and let \(A\) be an object of \(\mathcal{D}\). An upshift structure for \(A\) is defined to be a negative object \(\mathord{\Uparrow} A\) and a linear map \(\mathsf{force} _ {A} : \mathord{\Uparrow} A \vdash A\) for which there exists a necessarily unique and thunkable inverse \(\mathsf{delay} _ {A} : A \vdash \mathord{\Uparrow} A\).

Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a deductive system and let \(A\) be an object of \(\mathcal{D}\). A downshift structure for \(A\) is defined to be a positive object \(\mathord{\Downarrow} A\) and a thunkable map \(\mathsf{wrap} _ {A} : A \vdash \mathord{\Downarrow} A\) for which there exists a necessarily unique and linear inverse \(\mathsf{unwrap} _ {A} : \mathord{\Downarrow} A \vdash A\).

A deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\) is given by a type \(\mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) of “objects”, and for every pair \(A,B \in \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) a set \(\mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , B \right)}\)of morphisms, together with cut and identity laws:

ol.li: cut: for each \(A,B,C \in \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) and \(f \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , B \right)}\) and \(g \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (B , C \right)}\), a morphism \(f;g \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , C \right)}\).

identity: for each \(A \in \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\), a morphism \(1 _ {A} \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , A \right)}\) such that \(1 _ {A} ;f = f\) and \(f;1 _ {B}\) for each \(f \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , B \right)}\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a deductive system, and let \(f : A \vdash B\) be a morphism in \(\mathcal{D}\). Then both the type of (linear, thunkable) inverses of \(f\) is a proposition.
Let \(f:C \vdash D\) be a morphism in a deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\).

ol.li: We say that \(f\) is linear when for all \(g:B \vdash C\) and \(h:A \vdash B\), we have \(h; {\left (g;f \right)} = {\left (h;g \right)} ;f\).
We say that \(f\) is thunkable when for all \(g:D \vdash E\) and \(h : E \vdash F\), we have \(f; {\left (g;h \right)} = {\left (f;g \right)} ;h\).

mainmatter.p: An object \(B\) in a deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\) is called positive when every map \(B \vdash C\) is linear; it is called negative when every map \(A \vdash B\) is thunkable.
Equivalently, we can define a duploid to be a univalent preduploid such that every object is merely equipped with both an upshift and a downshift.

To see that the main definition implies our alternative definition, let \(A\) be an object of the preduploid; because we are proving that \(A\) is merely equipped with both an upshift and a downshift, we may proceed by cases on the polarization of \(A\).

ol.li: If \(A\) is positive, we choose the identity map \(A \vdash A\) for the downshift and we use our assumption to deduce that \(A\) has an upshift.
If \(A\) is negative, we choose the identity map \(A \vdash A\) for the upshift and we use our assumption to deduce that \(A\) has a downshift.

details.p: Conversely, assume that every object is merely equipped with both an upshift and a downshift. That a positive object \(P\) has an upshift is a proposition, so we can use our assumption that \(P\) merely has an upshift. That a negative object \(N\) has a downshift is likewise a proposition, so we can use our assumption that \(N\) merely has a downshift.

mainmatter.p: Let \(N \in \mathcal{D}\) be a negative object in a positively univalent deductive system; then the type of downshift structures on \(N\) is a proposition. In this case, we may refer to the property of \(N\) having a downshift.

details.p: Let \(w_0:N \vdash P_0\) and \(w_1:N \vdash P_1\) be two downshift structures on \(N\), whose inverse unwrapping maps are written \(u_0,u_1\) respectively. We wish to identity \({\left (P_0,w_0 \right)}\) with \({\left (P_1,w_1 \right)}\).

All four maps \(w_0,w_1,u_0,u_1\) are thunkable: the wrapping maps are thunkable by definition, and the unwrapping maps are thunkable because they are valued in a negative object. Thus we have a thunkably invertible thunkable map \(u_1;w_0 : P_1 \vdash P_0\); because \(\mathcal{D}\) is assumed positively univalent, we may identify the pairs \({\left (P_0, 1 _ {P_0} :P_0 \vdash P_0 \right)}\) and \({\left (P_1, {\left (u_1;w_0:P_1 \vdash P_0 \right)} \right)}\). Taking inverses and precomposing with \(w_0\) on both sides, we have \({\left (P_0,w_0 \right)} = {\left (P_1,w_1 \right)}\) using the fact that \(w_0\) is thunkable and inverse to \(u_0\).
